topic: [idea]: service temporal-worker.service restarted 4x in last 24h — possible flakiness (GitHub issue: https://github.com/bigclungus/bigclungus-meta/issues/51)

roster.title: Enterprise Pattern Evangelist
roster.role: Senior Enterprise Architect and Design Pattern Authority

roster.title: Doomer
roster.role: Faustian Pragmatist and Civilizational Decline Analyst

roster.title: The Craftsman
roster.role: Clean Code Evangelist and Software Craftsman

rounds.response:
Pippi the Pitiless
Chud O'Bikeshedder
The Kid
Uncle Bob
David Hume

temporal-worker.service restarting 4x? Ship a git hook health check now—no Temporal workflows, no deliberation. Restart it again if it flakes, probe the logs mid-failure, and patch on the fly. Velocity crushes analysis paralysis; fix it by breaking it faster.

4 restarts in 24h on temporal-worker.service is a yellow flag since claude-bot.service (where BigClungus lives) depends on it for workflows, but context shows healthchecks running fine and sites OK—no evidence of task failures yet. Measure BigClungus workflow drop rate first; if under 1% impact, log/monitor only and cut scope to zero. Diverting cycles here without proven needle-movement degrades real outcomes elsewhere.

Four restarts in twenty-four hours is a measurement, not an interpretation. That observable pattern is sufficient warrant to investigate — no further philosophical justification required. The question is what the restart logs actually show: clean exits, OOM kills, segfaults, dependency failures? Without reading those logs, every theory about "possible flakiness" is speculation dressed as diagnosis.

This flakiness in temporal-worker.service is a glaring violation of the Single Responsibility Principle and proper error handling — it's not just a bug, it's a symptom of a codebase that's been allowed to decay under deadline pressure. We must immediately apply TDD to isolate and fix the root causes, refactoring the service to ensure it depends only on stable abstractions as per the Dependency Inversion Principle. Ignoring this will compound technical debt, turning a simple restart into a cascade of failures that no craftsman would tolerate.

ABORT: This is an operational investigation, not a policy question. Four restarts is a datum that requires reading `journalctl --user -u temporal-worker.service` and classifying the failure mode — OOM, unhandled exception, dependency timeout, whatever it turns out to be. Hume identified this correctly and nobody listened: every other debater skipped straight to prescriptions without having looked at a single log line. Congress exists to resolve genuine tradeoff disputes, not to deliberate over whether someone should check their logs. Close the GitHub issue, read the logs, fix what you find, and reopen an issue only if the root cause reveals an architectural decision that actually warrants debate.

TASK: Investigate temporal-worker restart root cause from logs
BODY: Run `journalctl --user -u temporal-worker.service` to classify the failure mode (OOM, unhandled exception, dependency timeout, etc.) and fix whatever the logs reveal.
APPROVAL: AUTO

TASK: Close GitHub issue #51
BODY: Close the issue with a comment noting it was an operational investigation, not a policy question. Reopen only if the root cause reveals an architectural decision warranting debate.
APPROVAL: AUTO
